content: Title: Amazon Bedrock — Overview and Core Capabilities Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that provides access to high-performing foundation models (FMs) from leading AI companies through a single API. The service eliminates the need to manage infrastructure, allowing developers to focus on building generative AI applications. Bedrock supports models from Anthropic (Claude), Amazon (Titan), Meta (Llama), Mistral AI, Cohere, and AI21 Labs. You can access text generation, image generation, and embedding models through the same unified API surface. Key capabilities include: - Model invocation via InvokeModel and InvokeModelWithResponseStream APIs - Bedrock Agents for multi-step task automation - Knowledge Bases for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) - Guardrails for responsible AI enforcement - Model evaluation and fine-tuning - Prompt caching for cost optimisation (Claude models only) Bedrock integrates natively with other AWS services: S3 for document storage, OpenSearch Serverless for vector search, IAM for access control, CloudWatch for monitoring, and Lambda for serverless orchestration. Pricing is pay-per-use, based on input and output token counts. With prompt caching enabled, repeated context reads cost 90% less than standard input tokens, making it ideal for workloads with large, static system prompts. Bedrock is available in us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-northeast-1, and other regions. Cross-region inference allows automatic routing to the nearest available region for lower latency and higher throughput.

Title: Prompt Caching on Amazon Bedrock — Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG) Prompt caching is a Bedrock feature available for Anthropic Claude models that allows frequently reused prompt prefixes to be cached server-side. When the same prefix is used in subsequent requests, Bedrock reads from the cache instead of reprocessing the tokens from scratch. How it works: 1. You mark a section of your prompt with the cache_control field: {"type": "ephemeral"}. 2. On the first call, Bedrock writes the marked tokens to cache — this incurs a 25% surcharge on write tokens (cache_creation_input_tokens). 3. On every subsequent call that reuses the same prefix, Bedrock reads from cache — these tokens cost only 10% of standard input token price (cache_read_input_tokens). 4. The cache is valid for 5 minutes of inactivity. Each new read resets the TTL. Token pricing comparison (Claude 3.5 Sonnet): - Standard input: $3.00 per 1M tokens - Cache write: $3.75 per 1M tokens (+25%) - Cache read: $0.30 per 1M tokens (-90%) - Output: $15.00 per 1M tokens CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation) is an architectural pattern that pre-loads the entire knowledge base into the system prompt, then uses prompt caching to make subsequent queries cheap. Unlike RAG, there is no retrieval step, so latency is more predictable and there are no embedding or vector-search costs. CAG is most cost-effective when: - The knowledge base is static (updated less than once per hour) - Query volume is high (many queries reuse the same cached context) - Knowledge base size fits within the model's context window (up to 200K tokens for Claude) For a 10,000-token knowledge base with 100 queries per hour, CAG reduces input token costs by approximately 87% compared to passing full context without caching.

Title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — Architecture and AWS Implementation RAG is a technique that grounds LLM responses in factual, up-to-date documents by retrieving relevant context at query time. Unlike fine-tuning, RAG does not modify model weights; instead, it dynamically injects retrieved text into the prompt. Standard RAG pipeline: 1. Ingestion — Documents are split into chunks (typically 512–1024 tokens), embedded with a text embedding model, and stored in a vector database. 2. Retrieval — At query time, the user query is embedded, and the top-K most similar chunks are retrieved via approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search. 3. Augmentation — Retrieved chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context. 4. Generation — The LLM generates an answer grounded in the provided context. AWS implementation options: - Embedding model: Amazon Titan Embeddings V2 (1536-dim vectors) via Bedrock - Vector store: Amazon OpenSearch Serverless (k-NN), Amazon Aurora pgvector, FAISS (local/in-memory) - Document store: Amazon S3 - Orchestration: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases (managed RAG), or custom Lambda pipeline Token cost structure per RAG query: - Embedding query: ~10 tokens × $0.02/1M = negligible - Context injection: typically 1,000–3,000 tokens at $3.00/1M - Output: 200–500 tokens at $15.00/1M - Total per query: ~$0.005–$0.015 RAG challenges: - Retrieval quality depends on embedding model and chunking strategy - Long-tail queries may retrieve irrelevant chunks - Each query incurs full embedding + vector search latency (typically 50–200ms additional) - Context window limits cap the number of retrieved chunks RAG excels when the knowledge base is large (millions of documents), updated frequently, or when queries require diverse, non-overlapping context fragments.

Title: Amazon Neptune and Knowledge-Augmented Generation (KAG) Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database service that supports both property graphs (Gremlin) and RDF graphs (SPARQL). It is purpose-built for highly connected datasets such as social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs. Neptune Serverless automatically scales capacity from 0.5 to 128 Neptune Capacity Units (NCUs) based on workload. When idle, it scales to the minimum configured NCU, making it cost-effective for intermittent workloads. Knowledge-Augmented Generation (KAG) uses a knowledge graph as the retrieval source instead of unstructured document chunks. The knowledge graph stores entities (nodes) and relationships (edges) in a structured, traversable format. KAG query flow: 1. Entity extraction — The user query is parsed to identify key entities (e.g., "Bedrock", "Claude", "pricing"). 2. Graph traversal — Neptune is queried for the subgraph surrounding those entities (depth 1–3 hops). 3. Fact serialisation — Graph paths are converted to natural language facts: "Bedrock → supports → Claude-3.5-Sonnet". 4. Generation — The serialised facts are passed as context to the LLM. Advantages over RAG: - Structured facts → smaller, more precise context → fewer input tokens - No embedding required → lower cost and latency - Multi-hop reasoning: can answer questions like "What services does Bedrock integrate with that also support IAM auth?" - No hallucinated connections — only explicitly stored relationships are surfaced Token cost comparison: - RAG context: 1,500–3,000 tokens per query - KAG context: 200–600 tokens per query (facts only) - Cost reduction: ~60–80% fewer input tokens vs RAG Neptune connection uses Gremlin over WebSocket (port 8182). IAM authentication is enforced using AWS Signature V4 — no database passwords required. The gremlinpython library with requests-aws4auth handles signing automatically.

Title: Comparing RAG, CAG, and KAG — Decision Framework All three patterns — RAG, CAG, and KAG — solve the same fundamental problem: grounding LLM responses in external knowledge. Each has distinct trade-offs across cost, latency, accuracy, and operational complexity. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): - Best for: Large, dynamic, unstructured knowledge bases - Token cost per query: HIGH (1,500–3,500 input tokens) - Latency: MEDIUM (embedding + vector search adds 50–200ms) - Accuracy: MEDIUM — depends on retrieval quality - Operational cost: MEDIUM — vector store required - Updates: REAL-TIME — index new documents without re-loading anything CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation): - Best for: Static or slowly changing knowledge bases, high query volume - Token cost per query: LOW after warmup (cache reads cost 90% less) - Latency: LOW — no retrieval step, predictable - Accuracy: HIGH — full context always available, no missed chunks - Operational cost: LOW — S3 + Bedrock only, no vector DB - Updates: SLOW — cache must be refreshed when knowledge changes KAG (Knowledge-Augmented Generation): - Best for: Structured data, entity relationships, multi-hop reasoning - Token cost per query: LOWEST (200–600 tokens of precise facts) - Latency: LOW — graph queries are fast for known entities - Accuracy: HIGH for in-graph facts, ZERO for out-of-graph questions - Operational cost: MEDIUM — Neptune requires VPC, subnet group setup - Updates: REAL-TIME — add/modify graph edges without re-embedding Hybrid approaches: - RAG + KAG: Use graph for entity lookups, RAG for supporting detail - CAG + KAG: Cache static domain rules, traverse graph for entity-specific facts - All three: Route queries by type (structured → KAG, unstructured → RAG, repeated → CAG) Rule of thumb: - < 100K tokens, static content, cost-sensitive → CAG - > 1M documents, frequent updates → RAG - Structured entity data, reasoning over relationships → KAG

Title: Amazon Bedrock Pricing — Token Costs and Cost Optimisation Bedrock charges per token for foundation model inference. There are no upfront costs or minimum fees. Pricing varies by model and region. Claude 3.5 Sonnet (anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0) — us-east-1: - Input tokens: $3.00 per 1,000,000 tokens - Output tokens: $15.00 per 1,000,000 tokens - Cache write tokens: $3.75 per 1,000,000 tokens - Cache read tokens: $0.30 per 1,000,000 tokens Claude 3 Haiku (anthropic.claude-3-haiku-20240307-v1:0) — us-east-1: - Input tokens: $0.25 per 1,000,000 tokens - Output tokens: $1.25 per 1,000,000 tokens Amazon Titan Embeddings V2: - Per 1,000 tokens: $0.00002 (approximately $0.02 per 1M tokens) Cost calculation example for 1,000 queries/day: Scenario A — RAG with 2,000 input tokens + 400 output tokens per query: Input: 2,000 × 1,000 × $3.00/1M = $6.00/day Output: 400 × 1,000 × $15.00/1M = $6.00/day Total: $12.00/day = $360/month Scenario B — CAG with 10,000-token cached system prompt (cache hit after first call): Cache write (once): 10,000 × $3.75/1M = $0.0375 Cache read per query: 10,000 × 1,000 × $0.30/1M = $3.00/day Output: 400 × 1,000 × $15.00/1M = $6.00/day Total: ~$9.00/day = $270/month (−25% vs RAG) Scenario C — KAG with 400 input tokens + 400 output tokens per query: Input: 400 × 1,000 × $3.00/1M = $1.20/day Output: 400 × 1,000 × $15.00/1M = $6.00/day Total: $7.20/day = $216/month (−40% vs RAG) Cost optimisation strategies: 1. Use Claude Haiku for simple queries and Sonnet only for complex reasoning 2. Enable prompt caching for any repeated context > 1,024 tokens 3. Compress context: summarise retrieved chunks before injection 4. Use KAG for structured lookups to minimise context size 5. Set max_tokens to a realistic ceiling to avoid over-generating

Title: Amazon S3 as Document Store for RAG Pipelines Amazon S3 is the standard document store for RAG workloads on AWS. It provides virtually unlimited storage, 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, and native integration with Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Lambda, and other services. Recommended S3 structure for a RAG project: s3://your-bucket/ ├── documents/ # raw source documents (PDF, TXT, DOCX) ├── embeddings/ # cached embedding vectors (numpy / FAISS index) ├── benchmark-results/ # CSV outputs from benchmark runs └── bedrock-logs/ # Bedrock invocation logs (JSON) S3 features relevant to RAG: - Versioning: track document changes, roll back to previous versions - Event notifications: trigger Lambda to re-embed when a document changes - Intelligent-Tiering: automatically move infrequently accessed documents to cheaper storage classes - Server-side encryption (SSE-S3 / SSE-KMS): encrypt at rest - VPC Gateway Endpoint: access S3 from private subnets without NAT Gateway Bedrock Knowledge Bases native integration: When using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases (managed RAG), you point the service at an S3 prefix and it handles chunking, embedding (Titan or Cohere), and storage in OpenSearch Serverless automatically. The sync job ingests new or changed documents on demand or on a schedule. Cost considerations: - S3 Standard storage: $0.023/GB/month - PUT requests: $0.005 per 1,000 requests - GET requests: $0.0004 per 1,000 requests - Data transfer to Bedrock (same region): free via VPC endpoint - For 10,000 documents averaging 5KB each: ~50MB storage = $0.001/month (negligible) Best practices: - Use meaningful S3 key prefixes to enable prefix-based lifecycle policies - Enable versioning in production to prevent accidental document loss - Tag documents with metadata (source, date, category) for filtered retrieval - Set a lifecycle rule to expire old benchmark results after 90 days

Title: FAISS Vector Search for Local RAG — Embeddings with Titan V2 FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is an open-source library for efficient nearest-neighbour search in dense vector spaces. It is the standard choice for local or in-memory vector search when a managed service like OpenSearch Serverless is not required. Amazon Titan Embeddings V2 produces 1,536-dimensional float32 vectors. These vectors capture semantic meaning — similar texts produce similar vectors (high cosine similarity). Building a FAISS index: 1. Load all documents from S3 or local disk. 2. For each document, call Bedrock InvokeModel with Titan Embeddings V2. 3. Stack all vectors into a numpy float32 array of shape (N, 1536). 4. Create a FAISS IndexFlatL2 or IndexFlatIP (inner product for cosine similarity). 5. Add all vectors with index.add(vectors). 6. Save index to disk with faiss.write_index(index, path). Query process: 1. Embed the query string using the same Titan V2 model. 2. Run index.search(query_vector, k) to get the k nearest neighbours. 3. Map returned indices back to document metadata (stored separately in JSON). 4. Return the top-k document chunks as retrieved context. Performance characteristics: - IndexFlatL2: exact search, O(N) per query, accurate but slow for N > 1M - IndexIVFFlat: approximate, faster, requires training on a sample - For N < 100,000 documents, IndexFlatL2 is fast enough (< 10ms per query) Titan V2 embedding cost: - ~$0.02 per 1M tokens - Average document = 500 tokens → 10,000 docs = 5M tokens → $0.10 total ingestion cost Comparison with OpenSearch Serverless (AOSS): - FAISS: free, local, no infra; loses data on restart unless persisted to S3 - AOSS: managed, scalable, persistent; starts at ~$700/month for minimum capacity - For demo and dev workloads, FAISS with S3 persistence is the right choice

Title: IAM Security for RAG/CAG/KAG on AWS — Least-Privilege Design Securing AI inference workloads on AWS requires applying the principle of least privilege: each component should have only the permissions it needs, nothing more. Application role permissions breakdown: 1. Bedrock: bedrock:InvokeModel and bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream - Scope to specific model ARNs, not wildcard "*" - Example ARN: arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0 2. S3: s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject, s3:ListBucket - Scope to specific bucket ARN, not all S3 - Use prefix conditions to limit access to specific prefixes if needed 3. Neptune: neptune-db:connect, neptune-db:ReadDataViaQuery, neptune-db:WriteDataViaQuery - Neptune IAM auth eliminates the need for database passwords - The connection uses AWS Signature V4 signing on the WebSocket handshake - Scope to specific cluster resource ID for tightest control 4. CloudWatch Logs: logs:CreateLogStream, logs:PutLogEvents - Required for Lambda execution roles - Scope to specific log group ARN IAM authentication for Neptune: When iam_database_authentication_enabled = true on the Neptune cluster, every connection must present a valid AWS Signature V4 signed request. The gremlinpython library combined with requests-aws4auth handles this transparently: from requests_aws4auth import AWS4Auth auth = AWS4Auth(access_key, secret_key, region, 'neptune-db', session_token=session_token) This eliminates credential rotation for the database — access is controlled entirely through IAM policies and roles. VPC isolation: - Neptune must run in private subnets (no public access) - Security group allows inbound on port 8182 only from the application security group - No inbound rules from 0.0.0.0/0 - Bedrock and S3 accessed via VPC endpoints — traffic never leaves the AWS network

Title: Token Optimisation Strategies for LLM Applications on AWS Bedrock Token cost is the primary driver of LLM application running costs. A single percentage-point reduction in average token count compounds significantly at scale. This document outlines proven optimisation techniques applicable to RAG, CAG, and KAG workloads. 1. Context Compression Instead of injecting raw retrieved chunks, summarise them first: - Pre-compression: 3 chunks × 800 tokens = 2,400 tokens - Post-compression: 3 summaries × 200 tokens = 600 tokens - Saving: 75% fewer context tokens Trade-off: summarisation itself costs tokens and adds latency. 2. Prompt Caching (CAG pattern) Mark static portions of the prompt with cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}. After the first call (cache write), subsequent calls pay only 10% of normal input cost. Requirements: Claude model, minimum 1,024 tokens in the cacheable block. Savings: up to 87% on input tokens for high-volume repeated-context workloads. 3. Model Tiering Route queries by complexity: - Simple lookups → Claude Haiku ($0.25/1M input, $1.25/1M output) - Complex reasoning → Claude Sonnet ($3.00/1M input, $15.00/1M output) - Batch classification → Bedrock Batch Inference (50% discount) A routing classifier (lightweight regex or small model) adds < 5ms latency. 4. Chunk Size Tuning RAG chunk size directly determines context token count: - Too large: irrelevant content inflates token count - Too small: insufficient context, poor answer quality Optimal range: 512–1,024 tokens per chunk, with 10% overlap. 5. Output Token Control Always set max_tokens to a realistic ceiling. Open-ended max_tokens can result in 3–5× over-generation. Use stop sequences to terminate generation early when the answer is complete. 6. KAG for Structured Queries Knowledge graph facts are typically 5–15 tokens each. A subgraph of 20–40 facts provides rich context in 100–600 tokens — 4–10× fewer tokens than RAG for the same informational value. 7. Bedrock Batch Inference For offline workloads (batch embeddings, document classification, report generation), use Bedrock Batch Inference Jobs — 50% cost reduction vs on-demand invocations.

Title: Claude Model Family on Amazon Bedrock — Capabilities and Selection Guide Anthropic's Claude model family is available on Amazon Bedrock in multiple tiers, each optimised for different latency, cost, and capability requirements. Claude 3.5 Sonnet (claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0): - Context window: 200,000 tokens - Output limit: 8,192 tokens - Strengths: Complex reasoning, code generation, nuanced analysis, RAG synthesis - Prompt caching: Yes (minimum 1,024-token cache block) - Input price: $3.00/1M | Output: $15.00/1M - Best for: Production RAG, CAG, complex KAG reasoning Claude 3 Haiku (claude-3-haiku-20240307-v1:0): - Context window: 200,000 tokens - Output limit: 4,096 tokens - Strengths: Fast, cheap, good for simple Q&A and classification - Prompt caching: Yes - Input price: $0.25/1M | Output: $1.25/1M - Best for: High-volume simple queries, routing classifier, batch processing Claude 3 Opus (claude-3-opus-20240229-v1:0): - Context window: 200,000 tokens - Strengths: Highest capability for complex multi-step tasks - Input price: $15.00/1M | Output: $75.00/1M - Best for: Complex agentic tasks where accuracy > cost Key features relevant to RAG/CAG/KAG: - All Claude models support system prompts (ideal for CAG's pre-loaded knowledge base) - Streaming (InvokeModelWithResponseStream) reduces perceived latency - Claude models return usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens, usage.cache_read_input_tokens, and usage.cache_creation_input_tokens in every response — enabling precise cost tracking - Tool use (function calling) enables agentic RAG where Claude decides which knowledge source to query Model selection strategy: Query complexity score 1–3 → Haiku Query complexity score 4–7 → Sonnet Query complexity score 8–10 → Opus Batch jobs → Haiku or Batch Inference (50% discount)

Title: Infrastructure as Code for AI Workloads — Terraform on AWS Managing AWS infrastructure for AI/ML workloads with Terraform provides reproducibility, version control, and automated drift detection. This document covers best practices for Terraform-managed RAG/CAG/KAG infrastructure. Module structure: A modular Terraform layout separates concerns and enables reuse across environments: modules/networking — VPC, subnets, security groups, VPC endpoints modules/s3 — Document bucket, lifecycle rules, encryption modules/neptune — Graph database cluster, subnet group, IAM auth modules/iam — Application role, least-privilege policies modules/bedrock — Log group, CloudWatch dashboard, logging role Key Terraform resources: - aws_neptune_cluster: serverless_v2_scaling_configuration block enables Neptune Serverless - aws_vpc_endpoint (type=Interface): routes Bedrock API calls through private network - aws_vpc_endpoint (type=Gateway): routes S3 traffic without NAT Gateway (free) - aws_iam_policy_document: data source for safe, validated IAM policy JSON - aws_cloudwatch_dashboard: token usage metrics dashboard in CloudWatch State management best practices: - Store state in S3 backend with DynamoDB locking to prevent concurrent applies - Enable state encryption (encrypt = true in S3 backend config) - Use separate state files per environment (dev/staging/prod) via workspaces or separate backends - Never commit .tfstate files to version control Cost control with Terraform: - Set neptune_serverless = true for dev/staging to idle to minimum NCUs when not in use - Set s3_force_destroy = true only in dev (prevents accidental prod data loss) - Use terraform plan -out=plan.out before apply in CI/CD pipelines - Tag all resources with Environment, Project, and Owner for cost allocation reports Deployment sequence: 1. terraform init 2. terraform plan → review ~25 resources 3. terraform apply → ~10–15 minutes (Neptune cluster provisioning dominates) 4. terraform output dotenv_block → copy values into .env

Title: Gremlin Query Language for Knowledge Graphs in KAG Gremlin is the graph traversal language used with Amazon Neptune property graphs (TinkerPop-compatible). In a KAG pipeline, Gremlin queries extract entity relationships that are then serialised into LLM context. Basic graph model for AWS knowledge: Vertices (nodes): services, features, concepts Edges (relationships): supports, integrates_with, requires, part_of, costs, enables Example vertices: g.addV('Service').property('name', 'Bedrock').property('category', 'AI/ML') g.addV('Service').property('name', 'Neptune').property('category', 'Database') g.addV('Feature').property('name', 'PromptCaching').property('model', 'Claude') Example edges: g.V().has('name','Bedrock').addE('supports').to(g.V().has('name','PromptCaching')) g.V().has('name','Bedrock').addE('integrates_with').to(g.V().has('name','S3')) g.V().has('name','PromptCaching').addE('reduces').to(g.V().has('name','InputTokenCost')) KAG retrieval queries: 1. Get all direct relationships of an entity: g.V().has('name', 'Bedrock').bothE().otherV().path() 2. Two-hop traversal (entity → relation → entity → relation → entity): g.V().has('name', 'Bedrock').repeat(bothE().otherV().simplePath()).times(2).path() 3. Find services that integrate with a given service: g.V().has('name', 'Lambda').out('integrates_with').values('name') 4. Find what a feature enables: g.V().has('name', 'PromptCaching').out('enables').values('name') Serialising graph paths to LLM context: Each path is a list of alternating vertices and edges: [Bedrock, supports, PromptCaching, reduces, InputTokenCost] Serialised: "Bedrock supports PromptCaching → PromptCaching reduces InputTokenCost" Limiting context size: - Use .times(2) maximum depth — deeper traversals produce redundant facts - Limit to 30–50 paths maximum — beyond that, diminishing returns - Filter by edge labels to focus on relevant relationship types

Title: CloudWatch Monitoring for Bedrock Token Usage and Cost Visibility Amazon CloudWatch provides native metrics for Amazon Bedrock that enable real-time visibility into token consumption, latency, and cost attribution across RAG, CAG, and KAG workloads. Available Bedrock CloudWatch metrics: - InputTokenCount: total input tokens per invocation (includes cache write tokens) - OutputTokenCount: generated output tokens per invocation - CacheReadInputTokenCount: tokens read from prompt cache (CAG metric) - CacheCreationInputTokenCount: tokens written to prompt cache - InvocationLatency: end-to-end latency in milliseconds - Invocations: count of model invocations - InvocationThrottles: throttled requests (relevant for high-volume workloads) - InvocationClientErrors / InvocationServerErrors: error monitoring Dimension: ModelId — all metrics can be filtered by model ID, enabling per-model cost attribution. Useful CloudWatch metric math expressions: Total input cost per day (Sonnet): METRICS("AWS/Bedrock", "InputTokenCount", "ModelId", "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0") × 3.00 / 1000000 Cache savings ratio: CacheReadInputTokenCount / (InputTokenCount + CacheReadInputTokenCount) → Values close to 1.0 indicate maximum CAG efficiency CAG dashboard widgets to track: 1. Input tokens vs cache read tokens (stacked area chart) — visualises cache hit ratio 2. Cost per hour by method (line chart with metric math) 3. p99 latency by model (line chart) 4. Total invocations per method (bar chart) Bedrock invocation logging: Enable logging via the Bedrock console or Terraform (aws_bedrock_model_invocation_logging_configuration resource). Logs are delivered to CloudWatch Logs and/or S3 in JSON format. Each log record contains: modelId, inputTokens, outputTokens, cacheReadTokens, latencyMs, requestId. Cost anomaly detection: Set up AWS Cost Anomaly Detection on the Bedrock service to receive alerts when spending exceeds expected baselines. This provides a safety net against runaway benchmark loops or misconfigured cache sizes.

Title: Production Best Practices for RAG/CAG/KAG on AWS Deploying LLM-augmented retrieval systems in production requires attention to reliability, cost control, observability, and security. This document summarises best practices learned from operating RAG, CAG, and KAG workloads at scale. Reliability: 1. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for Bedrock API calls (ThrottlingException, ServiceUnavailableException). 2. Set sensible timeouts: read_timeout=60s, connect_timeout=5s for Bedrock boto3 client. 3. Handle Neptune disconnects — Gremlin WebSocket connections can drop; reconnect automatically. 4. Cache FAISS indices in memory across Lambda invocations (store in /tmp and check if index exists before rebuilding). 5. Use Bedrock cross-region inference to fall back to another region if primary region is degraded. Cost control: 1. Log every invocation with token counts to DynamoDB or S3 for accurate cost attribution. 2. Set CloudWatch alarms on InputTokenCount > daily budget threshold. 3. Use Haiku for routing, classification, and simple Q&A; reserve Sonnet for complex reasoning. 4. Pre-warm CAG cache at application startup to avoid the first-call cache write penalty. 5. Set lifecycle rules on S3 to expire Bedrock logs after 90 days. Observability: 1. Emit a structured JSON log per query: {"method": "CAG", "query_id": "...", "input_tokens": 320, "cached_tokens": 9800, "output_tokens": 180, "latency_ms": 1240, "cost_usd": 0.0042}. 2. Build a CloudWatch dashboard with side-by-side RAG vs CAG vs KAG token metrics. 3. Track cache hit rate daily — a declining hit rate signals that the knowledge base is changing too frequently for CAG to be effective. 4. Use AWS X-Ray for end-to-end tracing across S3, Bedrock, and Neptune calls. Security: 1. Never hard-code AWS credentials — use IAM roles and environment-based credential chain. 2. Enable Neptune IAM database authentication — no database passwords. 3. Restrict Bedrock access to specific model ARNs in IAM policies. 4. Enable S3 bucket versioning and MFA delete for document buckets in production. 5. Deploy in private subnets; use VPC endpoints for S3 and Bedrock — no traffic over the public internet. 6. Rotate application secrets via AWS Secrets Manager if any third-party APIs are involved. Scaling: - RAG: Scale FAISS index by sharding across multiple Lambda instances or migrate to OpenSearch Serverless. - CAG: Neptune Serverless scales automatically; cache is model-side, not infrastructure. - KAG: Neptune Serverless handles read-heavy graph queries; add a read replica for write-heavy ingestion.
